meta.name: Floodlight Visibility Catalog
meta.attackVersion: ATT&CK v18 (October 2025 — Detection Strategy / Log Sources model)
meta.scope: Curated high-value subset, not exhaustive. ~60 techniques anchored on Red Canary 2025 prevalence + CTID Top ATT&CK Techniques (prevalence, choke points, actionability), mapped to crown-jewel log sources with specific fields. Built to teach visibility-first posture, not to replace a detection-engineering program.
meta.identityEmphasis: Identity is the primary modern battleground, accelerated by AI: AiTM/token theft is now the top M365 breach vector, MFA is the target (bombing, federation/Golden SAML, help-desk resets), and non-human/AI-agent identities outnumber humans ~80:1. The catalog weights identity, cloud, and email sources accordingly and treats Identity Threat Detection & Response (ITDR) telemetry as crown-jewel.
meta.tempoEmphasis: Attacks are extremely accelerated: average eCrime breakout time is ~29 minutes (fastest 27 seconds), with AI-generated polymorphic, fileless, and LOLBin execution. Execution (TA0002) and Defense Evasion (TA0005) are flagged highTempo and weighted heavily. Critically, Defense Evasion attacks visibility itself — EDR killers via BYOVD (54 tools, 35 vulnerable drivers, 300+ EDR products terminable) blind endpoint telemetry — so the report must argue for breadth and redundancy: off-host log shipping, plus identity/cloud/network sources that survive endpoint blinding. Speed also raises the value of retention and fast, centralized collection.

sources.name: CTID Top ATT&CK Techniques (prevalence + choke points + actionability)
Red Canary Threat Detection Report — Top Techniques (2025 data)
Microsoft — Advanced security audit policy / event IDs

meta.disclaimer: Event IDs and field names reflect common Windows/Sysmon/cloud telemetry as of mid-2026. Names vary by platform and configuration; treat as the high-value starting set, not an authoritative per-environment mapping. Choke-point and prevalence flags are directional, drawn from CTID and Red Canary — not a guarantee of what targets a specific organization.

tactics.name: Reconnaissance
tactics.note: Occurs outside your environment — limited internal visibility by nature.

tactics.name: Resource Development
tactics.note: Adversary builds infrastructure off your network — not internally observable.

tactics.name: Execution
tactics.note: Convergence point and high-tempo — nearly every intrusion must run code, increasingly fileless / LOLBin / AI-generated. Process and script-block visibility here is among the highest-value you can have.

tactics.name: Defense Evasion
tactics.note: High-tempo and adversarial to visibility itself — EDR killers/BYOVD, rootkits, and log clearing attack your sensors. Catch it from off-host log shipping and sources that survive endpoint blinding.

tactics.name: Credential Access
tactics.note: Convergence point — credential theft funnels most hands-on-keyboard intrusions.

tactics.name: Lateral Movement
tactics.note: Convergence point — moving between hosts is hard to avoid and high-signal to catch.

tactics.name: Command and Control
tactics.note: Convergence point — C2 was the single most-observed tactic in 2025 telemetry.

logSources.name: Cloud / identity sign-in logs
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months minimum (identity is the new perimeter; longest practical retention preferred)
logSources.quality: Must include source IP, MFA result, auth method and conditional-access outcome — not just success/fail. Capture token/session issuance so AiTM session-token theft (now the primary M365 breach vector, ~31% in 2025) is detectable: a valid session with no fresh MFA, or reuse from a new IP/device, is the tell. Federated/SSO events must be correlated to the IdP, not the downstream app.

logSources.name: Directory / identity change audit
telemetry: Entra audit logs (Add member to role, Consent to application, Reset password, Update MFA)
Okta system log (help-desk MFA reset / recovery)
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months minimum; role/permission grants are slow-burn persistence.
logSources.quality: Capture the actor AND the target, plus OAuth consent scopes and role assignments. Help-desk-driven MFA resets and account-recovery changes are now a primary intrusion path (Scattered Spider) — these operations must be audited and alertable. Group/role changes without the granting principal are low value.

logSources.name: Non-human / AI-agent identity inventory & usage
telemetry: Entra service-principal sign-ins & app credential additions
AWS IAM credential report / Access Analyzer
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months; machine credentials are long-lived and abuse is slow to surface.
logSources.quality: Non-human identities (service accounts, API keys, OAuth apps, and now AI agents) outnumber humans roughly 80:1 and are typically over-privileged, unrotated, and unmonitored — the AI-era blind spot. Inventory them, track credential age and last-used, and alert on service-principal sign-ins from new IPs or sudden privilege scope changes.

logSources.name: Cloud management / API audit
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months minimum; control-plane abuse precedes most cloud incidents.
logSources.quality: Management events must be on in ALL regions/accounts. Data events (e.g. S3 object access) are separate and often disabled by default — enable for crown-jewel buckets.

logSources.name: Cloud object-storage data access logs
telemetry: AWS CloudTrail S3 data events / S3 server access logs
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months; primary evidence for cloud data theft (Red Canary #4, T1530).
logSources.quality: Data-event logging is OFF by default on most platforms and is the #1 cloud blind spot. Enable at least for sensitive buckets.

logSources.name: Process creation / execution telemetry
logSources.recommendedRetention: 6-12 months; command line is the single richest endpoint field.
logSources.quality: 4688 without command-line auditing is nearly worthless — the command line is the signal. Parent process and hash distinguish living-off-the-land abuse from benign use.

logSources.name: PowerShell / script-block logging
logSources.quality: Script-block logging (4104) captures deobfuscated content — essential for T1027 and T1059.001. Module logging alone misses obfuscated payloads.

logSources.name: Endpoint / domain authentication logs
logSources.quality: Logon type matters: type 10 (RDP), type 3 (network/SMB). 4769 with weak encryption flags Kerberoasting. Collect from domain controllers, not just endpoints.

logSources.name: Email gateway / message trace
telemetry: M365 message trace / Defender for Office
logSources.recommendedRetention: 6-12 months; phishing is still the dominant initial-access vector.
logSources.quality: Authentication results (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) and the final delivery action are what separate blocked from delivered. URL and attachment detonation verdicts add detection depth.

logSources.name: Mailbox / collaboration audit
telemetry: M365 Unified Audit Log (New-InboxRule, Set-InboxRule, UpdateInboxRules, Set-Mailbox, Add-MailboxPermission)
logSources.recommendedRetention: 12 months; inbox-rule abuse is top-10 prevalent (Red Canary #6 & #9).
logSources.quality: Mailbox auditing must be explicitly enabled and rule PARAMETERS captured (forward-to address, delete/move actions) — the rule name alone misses the intent.

logSources.name: Network traffic / flow logs
logSources.recommendedRetention: 3-6 months (high volume); aggregate/flow retained longer than full packet.
logSources.quality: Byte counts and direction enable exfil detection. East-west (internal) flow is the lateral-movement blind spot most orgs lack.

logSources.name: DNS query logs
logSources.quality: DNS is cheap to collect and high-value for C2/tunneling/DGA. Client-IP-to-query attribution is essential; resolver-only logs lose the originating host.

logSources.name: Web proxy / secure web gateway logs
logSources.quality: User attribution and full URL (not just domain) drive detection of tool transfer, web-service exfil, and RMM abuse.

logSources.name: File / object monitoring
logSources.quality: Mass file modification rate is the signature of ransomware (T1486). Share access (5145) supports lateral tool transfer detection.

logSources.name: Windows registry monitoring
logSources.quality: Scope to autostart, service, and security-tool keys — full registry auditing is noise. Process attribution is essential.

logSources.name: Scheduled task / service / WMI persistence
logSources.quality: Capture the command/image path and creating account — the task name is trivially spoofed.

logSources.name: Security log integrity / system events
logSources.quality: Log-clearing (1102/104) is a high-fidelity evasion signal — but only if logs are shipped off-host before they can be cleared.

logSources.name: EDR behavioral telemetry
telemetry: Sysmon 7/8/10 as a partial open-source substitute
logSources.quality: Cross-cuts many techniques (injection, credential dumping, defense evasion). The single highest-leverage endpoint investment, but does not replace identity/cloud/email visibility.

techniques.name: Phishing
techniques.note: Dominant initial-access vector; correlate delivered mail with subsequent execution.

techniques.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
techniques.note: Watch for web-server processes spawning shells/child processes.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts
techniques.note: Choke point. Legitimate credentials evade most controls — anomalous sign-in context is the tell.

techniques.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
techniques.note: Red Canary #1 (2025). Impossible-travel, new-device, and MFA-fatigue patterns in sign-in logs.

techniques.name: External Remote Services
techniques.note: VPN/RDP/gateway exposure; unsecured RDP led most 2025 ransomware cases.

techniques.name: Trusted Relationship
techniques.note: Supply-chain / third-party access abuse — watch external/partner principals.

techniques.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
techniques.note: Choke point. Command line is the signal.

techniques.name: PowerShell
techniques.note: Red Canary #2. Script-block logging (4104) captures deobfuscated content.

techniques.name: Windows Command Shell
techniques.note: Red Canary #3. cmd.exe with suspicious parent/child chains.

techniques.name: Windows Management Instrumentation
techniques.note: Red Canary #7. wmic/WMI for execution and lateral movement.

techniques.name: User Execution: Malicious Copy and Paste
techniques.note: Red Canary #8 (ClickFix). User pastes attacker command into Run/terminal.

techniques.name: Scheduled Task/Job
techniques.note: Execution + persistence; capture command and creating account.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Client Execution
techniques.note: Malicious document/browser exploit; office or browser process spawning child processes.

techniques.name: Native API
techniques.note: Fileless execution via direct API calls — bypasses command-line-only logging. EDR/in-memory telemetry required.

techniques.name: Software Deployment Tools
techniques.note: Abusing RMM/SCCM/Intune to push payloads to many hosts at once — a force multiplier for fast, wide compromise.

techniques.name: Inter-Process Communication
techniques.note: COM/DDE abuse for stealthy execution; office spawning anomalous children.

techniques.name: Create Account
techniques.note: New local/domain/cloud accounts as backdoor persistence.

techniques.name: Account Manipulation
techniques.note: Added credentials, roles, app permissions, mailbox rights.

techniques.name: Email Collection: Email Forwarding Rule
techniques.note: Red Canary #6. Auto-forwarding to external address; capture rule parameters.

techniques.name: Hide Artifacts: Email Hiding Rules
techniques.note: Red Canary #9. Rules that delete/move replies to hide BEC activity.

techniques.name: Server Software Component: Web Shell
techniques.note: Web-server process spawning shells; new files in web roots.

techniques.name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
techniques.note: Run keys, startup folder — scope registry auditing to autostart keys.

techniques.name: Create/Modify System Process: Windows Service
techniques.note: Service install (7045/4697) for persistence and SYSTEM-level execution.

techniques.name: Process Injection
techniques.note: Choke point. CreateRemoteThread/process access (Sysmon 8/10); EDR is primary.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
techniques.note: Anomalous child processes / token changes after exploit.

techniques.name: Domain or Tenant Policy Modification
techniques.note: GPO / conditional-access / federation tampering.

techniques.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
techniques.note: Red Canary #10. Encoded commands; 4104 deobfuscation is key.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal: Clear Windows Event Logs
techniques.note: 1102/104 — only catchable if logs ship off-host first.

techniques.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
techniques.note: EDR killers / BYOVD — 54 known tools abuse 35 vulnerable signed drivers to terminate 300+ EDR products before encryption. The sensor going silent IS the signal: alert on unexpected sensor stop, driver loads, and gaps in expected telemetry. This technique blinds endpoint visibility, so it must be caught from off-host log shipping and surviving identity/cloud/network sources.

techniques.name: Masquerading
techniques.note: Renamed system binaries; path/hash mismatch vs. process name.

techniques.name: System Binary Proxy Execution
techniques.note: LOLBins (rundll32, mshta, regsvr32) — parent/child + command line.

techniques.name: Modify Registry
techniques.note: Defense-evasion registry edits; scope to security-relevant keys.

techniques.name: Hijack Execution Flow (DLL sideloading)
techniques.note: DLL search-order hijacking / sideloading — a signed legit binary loads a malicious DLL. Module-load telemetry and file writes next to trusted binaries are the tell.

techniques.name: Reflective Code Loading
techniques.note: In-memory/fileless loading that leaves no file on disk — requires behavioral/memory telemetry; command-line and file logs miss it.

techniques.name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
techniques.note: AI-generated polymorphic/obfuscated payloads; 4104 script-block logging captures the decoded content at runtime.

techniques.name: Indicator Removal: File Deletion
techniques.note: Self-deletion of tooling/artifacts to frustrate forensics.

techniques.name: Subvert Trust Controls: Code Signing
techniques.note: Stolen/abused signing certs to make malware look trusted — signature presence is no longer sufficient; check signer reputation.

techniques.name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
techniques.note: Environment checks to dodge sandboxes/analysis before detonating.

techniques.name: Rootkit
techniques.note: Kernel-level (often via vulnerable driver) hiding of processes/files — watch driver loads and integrity of the boot/kernel chain.

techniques.name: Exploitation for Defense Evasion
techniques.note: Exploiting a flaw to disable or bypass a security control (overlaps BYOVD kernel exploitation).

techniques.name: OS Credential Dumping
techniques.note: Choke point. LSASS access (Sysmon 10) is the classic signal; EDR primary.

techniques.name: Brute Force
techniques.note: 4625 / failed sign-in bursts; password spray spans many accounts, few attempts each.

techniques.name: Kerberoasting
techniques.note: 4769 service-ticket requests with weak (RC4) encryption.

techniques.name: Steal Application Access Token
techniques.note: Red Canary featured. Illicit OAuth consent grants and token theft.

techniques.name: Credentials from Password Stores
techniques.note: Browser/credential-manager access; infostealer behavior.

techniques.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
techniques.note: AiTM session-token theft — now the primary M365 breach vector (~31% in 2025). Valid session with no fresh MFA, reused from a new IP/device.

techniques.name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation
techniques.note: MFA-fatigue / push bombing. Bursts of MFA prompts to a user, especially from unusual IPs not matching a user-initiated login.

techniques.name: Modify Authentication Process
techniques.note: Disabling/weakening MFA, altering conditional access, or tampering with federation to persist. Includes T1556.006 (MFA).

techniques.name: Forge Web Credentials: SAML Tokens
techniques.note: Golden SAML — forging tokens with a stolen signing certificate to impersonate any user across federated services. Watch token-signing cert access and anomalous SAML issuance.

techniques.name: Account Discovery
techniques.note: net user / AD enumeration; BloodHound-style collection.

techniques.name: Remote System Discovery
techniques.note: Network scanning / host enumeration from a single source.

techniques.name: Domain Trust Discovery
techniques.note: Trust enumeration preceding cross-domain movement.

techniques.name: Remote Services
techniques.note: Choke point. RDP (logon type 10), SMB (type 3), WinRM between hosts.

techniques.name: Remote Services: RDP
techniques.note: Logon type 10; unusual source→dest RDP pairs.

techniques.name: Lateral Tool Transfer
techniques.note: Admin-share writes (5140/5145) of tooling between hosts.

techniques.name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
techniques.note: Pass-the-hash / stolen session cookies; anomalous auth material reuse.

techniques.name: Data from Cloud Storage
techniques.note: Red Canary #4. Mass object reads; requires data-event logging enabled.

techniques.name: Email Collection
techniques.note: Bulk mailbox access/export; correlate with forwarding rules.

techniques.name: Data from Information Repositories
techniques.note: SharePoint/Confluence/wiki mass access by a single principal.

techniques.name: Archive Collected Data
techniques.note: rar/7z/zip staging before exfil.

techniques.name: Application Layer Protocol
techniques.note: Choke point. C2 over HTTP(S)/DNS; C2 was the most-observed tactic in 2025.

techniques.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
techniques.note: Red Canary #5 + choke point. Downloading payloads/tools post-access.

techniques.name: Protocol Tunneling
techniques.note: DNS/ICMP/SSH tunneling; long-lived or high-entropy flows.

techniques.name: Remote Access Software
techniques.note: RMM abuse (AnyDesk/ScreenConnect/etc.) for hands-on access.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
techniques.note: Outbound byte volume on established C2; direction matters.

techniques.name: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.note: Uploads to mega/dropbox/etc.; unusual destination + volume.

techniques.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
techniques.note: DNS/FTP/non-standard-port exfil bypassing the proxy.

techniques.name: Data Encrypted for Impact
techniques.note: Ransomware signature. Mass file-modification rate + shadow-copy deletion.

techniques.name: Inhibit System Recovery
techniques.note: vssadmin/wbadmin delete; precedes encryption — a late-stage warning.

techniques.name: Service Stop
techniques.note: Stopping DBs/backups/security services before impact.

techniques.name: Data Destruction
techniques.note: Wiper behavior; mass deletion distinct from encryption.

retentionByRegulation.name: PCI DSS 4.0 (Req. 10.5.1)
retentionByRegulation.retention: 12 months total; most recent 3 months immediately available

retentionByRegulation.name: HIPAA Security Rule
retentionByRegulation.retention: 6 years for required documentation; audit logs commonly retained 6 years by extension

retentionByRegulation.name: OMB M-21-31
retentionByRegulation.applies: US federal agencies (and many federal contractors by flow-down)
retentionByRegulation.retention: 30 months total — 12 months active (hot) + 18 months cold storage

retentionByRegulation.name: NERC CIP-007
retentionByRegulation.applies: Bulk electric system / North American power utilities
retentionByRegulation.retention: Minimum last 90 consecutive calendar days of security event logs

retentionByRegulation.name: SOC 2 / common audit
retentionByRegulation.retention: Commonly 12 months; set by control objectives, not a fixed statute
retentionByRegulation.citation: AICPA Trust Services Criteria (no fixed period)

retentionByRegulation.name: GDPR / CCPA (tension)
retentionByRegulation.retention: Data minimization — retain only as long as necessary; security logs justifiable under legitimate interest but must be scoped and access-controlled
retentionByRegulation.citation: GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) & (e); note the detection-vs-minimization tension

motivationProfiles.name: Financially motivated / ransomware
motivationProfiles.note: The default threat for nearly every organization. Initial access via phishing/RDP/valid accounts, then encrypt-and-extort.

motivationProfiles.name: State-sponsored / espionage
motivationProfiles.note: Stealth and persistence over impact. Relevant to defense, government, critical infrastructure, high tech, and their suppliers.

motivationProfiles.name: Hacktivism / disruption
motivationProfiles.note: Public-facing exploitation and defacement/DDoS/data-destruction; spikes with geopolitical or reputational triggers.

motivationProfiles.name: Insider threat
motivationProfiles.note: Legitimate access abused; identity, data-access, and exfil logs carry the signal.

motivationProfiles.name: Supply-chain / third-party
motivationProfiles.note: Compromise via trusted vendor/software. Watch external principals and control-plane changes (T1199).
